questions.source: Systems Architecture video (Gg318hR5JY0) SA-V4; AOSA distsys
questions.question: You are designing a photo archive where users rarely access images older than one year, but must be able to retrieve them within seconds when requested. Which storage trade-off best matches this access pattern?
options: Store hot recent photos in flash/SSD and tier older photos to cheaper rotating disk
Store all photos in memory for uniform low latency regardless of age
Use only rotating disk everywhere because it is the cheapest per gigabyte
Replicate every photo to flash in every region to eliminate latency variance
questions.explanation: The official Systems Architecture prep video emphasizes that rotating disk offers cheaper storage at higher latency, while flash/memory trade cost for speed. Tiering matches infrequent old access with cost efficiency while keeping recent data fast — a classic trade-off interviewers expect you to articulate explicitly.

questions.question: A social feed service needs fast reads of denormalized timelines keyed by user ID, tolerates eventual consistency, and rarely performs cross-user joins. Which database trade-off is most appropriate to discuss first?
options: A wide-column or document store optimized for key-based reads over normalized SQL joins
A fully normalized relational schema with strict ACID across all feed tables
An in-memory-only store with no persistence because reads dominate
A graph database because social networks are inherently graphs
questions.explanation: SQL vs NoSQL is not about 'correctness' but access pattern fit. Timeline reads by user ID map naturally to key-value or wide-column designs with denormalized rows. SQL excels at complex joins and strong consistency — less critical here. Interviewers want you to match the datastore to read/write shape and consistency needs.

questions.question: An e-commerce checkout must confirm payment before showing an order receipt, but sending a confirmation email can wait. How should you frame the sync vs async trade-off?
options: Process payment synchronously on the critical path; enqueue email delivery asynchronously
Make both payment and email fully synchronous so the user sees end-to-end consistency
Make payment asynchronous and poll for completion to reduce load on the payment gateway
Skip email entirely in v1 because async adds unacceptable complexity
questions.explanation: Sync processing preserves user-visible correctness on the critical path (payment confirmed before receipt). Async decouples non-critical work (email) to improve latency and resilience. The trade-off is coupling vs responsiveness — a coherent design separates what the user must wait for from what can happen later.

questions.question: You need a durable work queue for order-fulfillment events that must survive consumer restarts, support replay, and handle high throughput with partition-level ordering. Kafka vs a database-backed queue — which trade-off favors Kafka?
options: Kafka's log-based, partitioned design gives replay and ordering at scale; a DB queue simplifies ops but bottlenecks on DB write throughput
A DB queue is always superior because transactions guarantee exactly-once without extra infrastructure
Kafka eliminates the need for any persistence layer because consumers hold all state
Neither is appropriate; synchronous RPC between services is simpler and equally scalable
questions.explanation: Kafka trades operational complexity for scalable, ordered, replayable streams. DB queues reuse existing infrastructure but often become write-limited and costly at high volume. The interview answer is not 'always Kafka' — it is explaining when log semantics and partition ordering justify the added system.

questions.question: A read-heavy product catalog page displays product name, price, inventory count, and seller rating together. Queries run thousands of times per second. Which normalization trade-off is most defensible?
options: Denormalize frequently co-read fields into a single document or row to avoid joins on every page load
Fully normalize into five tables and join on every read to guarantee zero write amplification
Store only product IDs and fetch each attribute from a separate microservice synchronously
Avoid any schema design until traffic exceeds one million QPS
questions.explanation: Normalization reduces update anomalies; denormalization reduces read latency and join cost. For read-heavy, co-displayed data, controlled denormalization with explicit invalidation strategy is a standard trade-off. Interviewers score whether you can articulate the write-amplification cost you accept for read performance.

questions.source: Systems Architecture video SA-V3; AOSA distsys
questions.question: Rough latency hierarchy from fastest to slowest for a single random read in a well-provisioned system is typically:
options: CPU cache / main memory → SSD/flash → rotating disk → cross-continent network RTT
Rotating disk → main memory → SSD → cross-continent network RTT
Cross-continent network RTT → SSD → main memory → rotating disk
All storage tiers have equivalent latency if you use a load balancer
questions.explanation: The Systems Architecture prep video stresses quantitative intuition: memory reads are orders of magnitude faster than disk, and local network RTT is far cheaper than cross-continental hops. Knowing this hierarchy lets you justify caching, tiering, and regional placement — not memorize exact nanoseconds.

questions.question: During a system design interview, you propose Cassandra for a workload that clearly needs strong relational joins. The interviewer asks why. What demonstrates the strongest trade-off thinking?
options: Acknowledge the mismatch, explain why another store fits the access pattern better, and describe what you would sacrifice or gain with each option
Insist Cassandra is always the right choice at Google scale regardless of access pattern
Switch topics to sharding without addressing the datastore concern
Ask to skip storage design because it is not part of the rubric
questions.explanation: The recruiter email explicitly states that implications matter more than a single 'correct' choice and that a coherent design beats memorized answers. Acknowledging trade-offs, rejected alternatives, and consistency with requirements shows mature engineering judgment — exactly what B6 evaluates.

questions.question: Static assets (JavaScript bundles, images) for a global web app must load quickly worldwide. Which CDN trade-off should you describe?
options: Push popular assets to edge caches proactively; use longer TTLs and cache invalidation for updates
Pull every asset from origin on each user request to guarantee freshness
Disable CDN caching entirely to simplify debugging during the interview
Store assets only in the primary datacenter and rely on users' ISP caching
questions.explanation: CDN push (or prefetch) vs pull is a latency/freshness trade-off. Static assets with versioned filenames tolerate long TTLs at the edge, reducing origin load and cross-region RTT. Pull-on-miss is simpler for highly dynamic content. Articulating when to invalidate vs version assets shows practical CDN thinking.

questions.question: A collaborative document editor can show a teammate's cursor with a 200 ms delay but must never lose committed text edits. Which trade-off framing is most appropriate?
options: Strong consistency/durability for committed edits; eventual consistency acceptable for ephemeral presence data
Strong consistency for all data including cursor positions to eliminate any visual lag
Eventual consistency everywhere to maximize availability during network partitions
No consistency model discussion is needed if you shard by document ID
questions.explanation: Different data within one product carries different consistency requirements. Committed edits need durability and strong guarantees; presence/cursor data is latency-sensitive and tolerates staleness. Splitting requirements avoids a false binary of 'CP vs AP' for the entire system — a hallmark of good trade-off analysis.

questions.question: You have time to present one v1 architecture and one improvement pass. What best satisfies the 'trade-offs and compromises' expectation from the official Systems Architecture video?
options: Present a workable v1, name two systematic shortcomings, propose a concrete v2 change, and state what you traded away
Deliver only the final perfect design without mentioning alternatives to save time
List ten possible databases without committing to any design
Focus exclusively on failure modes before establishing any baseline architecture
questions.explanation: SA-V4 explicitly asks candidates to identify shortcomings, lay out trade-offs (e.g., disk cost vs latency), consider multiple solutions, commit to one, and iterate. Documenting rejected alternatives and their costs demonstrates the iterative, compromise-aware process Google scores — not a single immutable 'correct' diagram.
